title: Respect prefers-reduced-motion
summary: AI-generated animations often lack reduced-motion media query support, ignoring vestibular accessibility needs.
instruction: Wrap non-essential animations in a prefers-reduced-motion media query. Provide a motion-reduced alternative that preserves the interaction's functional outcome without visual movement.
rationale: Motion can cause vestibular discomfort, nausea, and disorientation for a significant portion of users. WCAG requires a mechanism to pause, stop, or hide motion. A proper reduced-motion implementation protects accessibility without banning motion entirely.
violations: A hero section with a 5-second looping video background, parallax scrolling, and animated text reveals - none wrapped in prefers-reduced-motion.
A dashboard with animated chart transitions, spinning loading indicators, and sliding notifications that cannot be disabled.
controls: A marketing page with CSS transitions that respect prefers-reduced-motion: reduce, replacing slide animations with crossfade or instant state changes.
A presentation tool with a global 'reduce motion' toggle that sets prefers-reduced-motion on the document, disabling non-essential animations.
borderlineCases: Essential animations (progress bars, loading spinners that indicate activity) may need to remain visible in reduced-motion mode, but should stop looping or reduce speed.
contextualExceptions: Critical-system alerts that use animation to draw attention.
Video content (user-watched, not auto-playing decorated video).
detectorSignal: CSS animations, transitions, or keyframes without an associated prefers-reduced-motion override
matcherProposal: Search for CSS animation, transition, @keyframes declarations. Check for @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce/no-preference) wrappers. If animation exists without reduction, flag. Also check for JavaScript Web Animation API calls without motion preference checks.
detectorLimitations: Cannot detect motion added via JavaScript libraries (GSAP, Framer Motion) without static source analysis.
JavaScript event listeners for animation may not appear in static analysis.
falsePositiveScenarios: A page using only CSS transitions for hover effects that are essential to interaction feedback (button press, link hover) - these are typically exempt.
Animations that only play once on page load (not looping) may be low-risk but should still be checked.
mitigationStrategy: Flag only non-essential, looping, or continuous animations without reduction. Exclude micro-interactions (hover states, button presses, focus transitions).

fixturePlans.violation: A landing page with @keyframes float, @keyframes fadeInUp, CSS transitions on all elements, and no prefers-reduced-motion anywhere.
fixturePlans.control: A landing page with the same effects but wrapped in @media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference), with a reduced alternative using @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce).
fixturePlans.borderline: A page with only button hover scale(1.02) transitions and a loading spinner that stops after data loads (non-looping essential feedback).

cleanRoomAttestations: WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 2.3.3 (Animation from Interactions) and 2.2.2 (Pause, Stop, Hide).
overlapNotes: Complementary to motion.transition-all (targets property-level animation overuse). This targets accessibility compliance for reduced-motion preferences.
